text: The surgeon carefully dissects and removes omental adhesions from the inflamed gallbladder, ensuring the transverse colon is not injured during the process. Indocyanine green (ICG) contrast is visible, concentrated in the liver due to common bile duct obstruction.

The surgeon carefully dissects the duodenum, which is adherent to the inflamed gallbladder, using the cut mode. The Firefly mode is activated, revealing the common bile duct with altered intensity against the background.

The surgeon exchanges instruments, moving fundus retraction to port number four. The critical view of safety is achieved, and fiber fatty tissue is cleared from the hepatocystic triangle. The common bile duct is exposed to assist with trans-cystic CBD exploration. A clip is placed below the infundibulum after milking the cystic duct towards the gallbladder. The cystic duct is incised, and minor bleeding is controlled.

The surgeon inserts a five French cholangiocatheter through an introducer, using wires to stiffen it for easier passage through the cystic duct. The C-arm is positioned from the patient's right side, with arm one and arm two undocked to accommodate its placement. Contrast is injected through the cholangiocatheter, revealing filling defects in the common bile duct and showing contrast flow to the duodenum. The proximity of the cystic duct to the common bile duct obstruction is visible.

The surgeon upgrades from the cholangiogram catheter to a Cook common bile duct exploration set, inserting a dilator into the sheath. After removing the dilator, the sheath remains in place, and a balloon is inserted into the cystic duct. The balloon insertion encounters difficulty due to a stone positioned adjacent to the common bile duct.

The surgeon attempts to pass the dilator and inflates the balloon within the cystic duct, maintaining inflation for three minutes to dilate the duct to approximately eight millimeters. After deflation, the choledochoscope is inserted to examine the proximal part, revealing the bifurcation of the right and left hepatic branches. Blood is visible from the scope insertion. The choledochoscope is advanced distally, revealing a stone. A decision is made to perform direct common bile duct exploration by opening the duct, using a four French balloon to dislodge the stone distally.

The surgeon pulls on the duodenum, causing it to rise, and performs multiple passes to clear fat from the top of the common bile duct. The falciform ligament is lifted for retraction. The cystic duct is clipped, revealing a view of the stone in the common bile duct. The cholecystectomy is completed, and the gallbladder is placed in a bag left in situ to prevent interference with visualization.

The surgeon carefully dissects fatty tissue from the top of the common bile duct, revealing the hepatic artery to the left, which is deeper than the common bile duct. Using the cut mode, the common bile duct is incised, immediately exposing a stone. Irrigation is applied to enhance visualization and facilitate the removal of floating stones.

The surgeon extends the incision in the common bile duct to accommodate larger stones. A small bleeder is identified and controlled before coagulation. Seven stones are removed from the common bile duct, aided by irrigation that helps float the stones for easier removal.

A stone is extracted from the distal common bile duct using irrigation. The surgeon milks the distal common bile duct to bring stones into view, ensuring they are collected in one place. Continued irrigation is performed, and a choledochoscope is used to inspect the duct for clearance before further irrigation with a catheter.

The surgeon uses an interrupted PDS suture to close the incision in the common bile duct, preferring a large needle driver for this task. The distal end of the incision is identified, and sutures are placed at the most distal end of the choledotomy.

The surgeon completes the closure of the common bile duct and confirms duct clearance with a cholangiogram. The common bile duct appears dilated, measuring up to 1.5 centimeters. A clip remover is used to remove clips from the cystic duct without damaging the tissue. The catheter is inserted into the cystic duct, and the final cholangiogram shows good flow to the proximal hepatic branches and distal common bile duct into the duodenum.
